title: Writing Your Talk Show Script
content: writing talk show script use following guide script. print page work directly write separate sheet modify wording format necessary. use documents "daughters free men" viewer's guide (http://www.ashp.cuny.edu/video/d-guide.html) video decide guests viewpoints represent. introduction moderator host: good morning afternoon/ evening. americans recently concerned disturbances textile mills lowell, massachusetts. i'm _____________ you're watching _________________.today/tonight we'll talking _______________. better understand events, we'll talk ____________________, is___________; well as______________________, who_______________________. finally, perspective _____________________, we'll also have_______________________on show. going start _______________. mr/mrs.__________________, tell us __________. 1st guest: well, ________________________________________________________. host's response: let's go ________________________________for response. mr/mrs._________________, think ____________'s comment? 2nd guest: well, ____________________________________________________ 1st guest response 2nd guest: _______________________________________ continue talk show/round table discussion format, involving guests dialogue/debate shows different sides issues involved: _____________________________________________________________________. concluding statement host: ____________________________________________. talk show script template developed written joel ortiz, san diego state university.

answered englishmen shoot kill—if shoot, kill. ordered us come time boarded us, obliged surrender without conditions. proceeded bind four men master, capt. bovey one. stripped us, one clothes, leaving tie greasy shirt waistcoat, drawers used fish shoes stockings cabin. gave us liberty sit upon deck, bound till two clock afternoon. unbound us, commanded us sail vessel towards penobscot, endeavored wind shortening forced come anchor lay till second day capture. meantime, told us intended kill us, englishmen, number twenty six, including boys, except three. taken four vessels besides second day commanded us ketches sail together penobscot. indians dispersed ketches; seventy eighty sailed onward espied bark gave chase soon took found mr. watts vessel. indians compelled us haile answered us boston, bound fishing voyage. prevent murder men, soon came told taken, thinking joke, laughed us. indians rose told capt watts strike dead men. four indians went board divided mixed englishmen different vessels sending master bovey one man company, onboard another ketch, left master ketch, wholly disliking bovey) old man, desired. board capt. watts, indians sent two number away, took two capt. watts' men place, whereof one william buswell.we thus situated short time, another sail discovered, commanded give chase. till began grow disky [dusky], indian sagamore vessel ordered helm, bear refused. thereupon sagamore grew angry, fall upon william buswell observing, seized throat, close scuffle ensued. buswell however soon tripped heels, fell upon kept knee upon breast. meantime, another companions captivity, named richard dowries, closing second indian, succeeded getting also;

title: "What Can You Get By Warre": Powhatan Exchanges Views With Captain John Smith, 1608"
content: home many pasts evidence www.history blackboard reference talking history syllabi students teachers puzzle us search site get warre”: powhatan exchanges views captain john smith, 1608" captain john smith soldier adventurer europe asia became involved virginia company’s plan establish settlement north america. aboard one three ships reached virginia april 1607. first settlers, ill prepared life harsh environment, useful skills great expectations easy profits. suffered disease, malnutrition, frequent attacks indians early years; one half died first winter. smith took jamestown’s government amid chaos death; explored region traded desperately needed supplies indians. smith recognized need establish peaceful relations powerful powhatan indians coastal region, traded english manufactured goods much needed indian corn. smith recounted exchange indian leader powhatan 1624 historyie. [powhatan:]captaine smith, may understand seene death people thrice, one living three generations selfe; know difference peace warre better country. old ere long must die, brethren, namely opitchapam, opechancanough, kekataugh, two sisters, two daughters, distinctly others successors. wish experience lesse mine, love lesse mine bruit nandsamund, come destroy country, much affrighteth people dare visit availe take force may quickly love, destroy provide food. get warre, hide provisions fly woods? whereby must famish wronging us friends. thus jealous loves seeing us unarmed, doe, willing still feede cannot get labours? thinke simple, know better eate good meate, lye well, sleepe quietly women children, laugh merry copper, hatchets, want friend: forced flie lie cold woods, feede upon acornes, rootes, trash, hunted neither rest, eate, sleepe; tyred men must watch, twig breake, every one cryeth commeth captaine smith: must fly know whether: thus miserable feare, end miserable life, leaving pleasures youths rash unadvisednesse may quickly miserably end, want never know finde. let therefore assure loves, every yeare friendly trade shall furnish corne; also, would come friendly manner see us, thus guns swords invade foes. subtill discourse, president thus replyed.capt. smiths reply. seeing rightly conceive words, strive make know thoughts deeds; vow made love, selfe men kept. promise find every day violated subjects: yet finding love kindnesse, custome far ungratefull, sake onely, curbed thirsting desire revenge; els knowne well crueltie use enemies, true love courtesie friends. thinke judgement sufficient conceive, well adventures undertaken, advantage armes) intended hurt, long ere could effected people comming james towne entertained bowes arrowes without exceptions; esteeming us, weare armes apparell. danger enemies, warres consist chiefest pleasure: riches use: hiding provision, flying woods, shall unadvisedly starve conclude, friendly care behalfe needlesse, rule finde beyond knowledge.many discourses till last began trade. king seeing would admitted law, guard dispersed, men disarmed, (sighing) breathed minde manner.source: john smith, generall historie virginia, new england summer isles (glasgow, scotland: james maclehose sons, 1907), vol. 1: 158–59

(b week) feb. 10: born liberty, pp 125t-143. feb. 11: people, pp 384-392. feb. 12: people, pp 393-400. feb. 13: in-service day. classes. read slavery?," pp 6t-12. week february 16 feb. 16: holiday. slavery?," pp 1-6t. feb. 17: first draft term paper due. summary review slavery?". feb. 18: people, pp 401-408b. feb. 19: people, pp 413t-418. feb. 20: born liberty, pp 145-160m. week february 23 feb. 23: review mid-term examination. feb. 24: mid-term examination. feb. 25: people, pp 419-420b; 425vb-436. feb. 26: people, pp 438-447m. feb. 27: people, pp 447m-456. week march 2 march 2: video: moving north chicago. (b week) march 3: final draft term paper due. march 4: born liberty, pp 160m-173. march 5: born liberty, pp 175-186. march 6: born liberty, pp 186-196 people, pp 465t-468t. week march 9 march 9: people, pp 458-465t. march 10: people, pp 468t-475. march 11: rise ghetto, pp 1-6. march 12: lecture: harlem renaissance. march 13: people, pp 476-483t. fourth quarter assignments week march 30 march 30: people, pp 483t-490t. march 31: people, pp 490t-492; 496vb-498. (introduction ohp) april 1: born liberty, pp 197-210b. april 2: born liberty, pp 210b-218 people, pp 493-496. april 3: people, pp 499-508t. week april 6 april 6: people, pp 508t-515. april 7: people, pp 517-524t; 530-534. april 8: video: homefront (double period). people, pp 524t-530t. april 9: born liberty, pp 219-234t. april 10: people, pp 536-546m. week april 13 april 13: discussion people, pp 536-546m. (b week) (journal entry video due). april 14: people, pp 546m-552. april 15: people, pp 554-562m. lecture: changing roles women. april 16: people, pp 562m-570b. april 17: people, pp 570b-577. (summary review). week april 20 april 20: review mid-term examination. april 21: mid-term examination. april 22: born liberty, pp 234t-241. april 23: born liberty, pp 243-262. april 24: born liberty, pp 263-273b. week april 27 april 27: born liberty, pp 273b-285. april 28: born liberty, pp 287-301m; 304t-307b. lecture: women, work family. april 29: born liberty, pp 301m-304t; 307b-314. hand simulation characters. april 30: people, pp 594-603t. lecture: vietnam war. may 1: people, pp 603t-611b. week may 4 may 4: people, pp 611b-618. (summary review). may 5: people, pp 620-629t. may 6: people, pp 629t-636m. may 7: video: sixties. may 8: video: sixties. week may 11 may 11: music sixties. paper sim4 due. may 12: video: malcolm x. may 13: work oral history project. may 14: work oral history project. may 15: lecture: changing patterns immigration week may 18 may 18: oral history presentations. may 19: oral history presentations. may 20: review final examination. may 21: review final examination. may 22: review final examination.

content: home many pasts evidence www.history blackboard reference talking history syllabi students teachers puzzle us search site back talking history results back archive list date: fri, 30 nov 2001 16:51:36 -0500 reply-to: american indians forum sender: american indians forum educate subject: wmm new release: kinaalda mime-version: 1.0 content-type: text/plain; charset="iso-8859-1" wmm new release women make movies proud announce new release kinaalda: navajo rite passage lena carr. kinaalda ceremony intricate four-day event performed guide young girls' ascent womanhood. unique documentary, navajo filmmaker lena carr journeys back childhood chronicling 13-year old niece's initiation womanhood. festival international du film d'amiens, amiens, france santa fe film festival american indian film festival museum american indian film festival multi-layered film elegantly weaves carr's complex personal story, family community history, backdrop u.s. government policy toward navajo... compelling work beginning end." elaine charnov, margaret mead film festival moving documentary...it examines painful history navajo tribe shows decisions deceptions long ago effect tribe today." women director's chair pricing ordering info kinaalda: navajo rite passage film lena carr 56 minutes/color, video/subtitled rental $75 vhs sale $250 order 02762 order kinaalda, please visit website http://www.wmm.com/catalog/pages/c556.htm, contact us women make movies 462 broadway, suite 500 new york, ny 10013 p/212.925.0606 x360, f/212.925.2052 orders@wmm.com wmm nearly 30 years, women make movies largest distributor films videotapes women world. provide diverse collection 400 titles viewing public supported past grants macarthur foundation national endowment arts. catalogue used thousands educational, community cultural organizations annually. forum sponsored history matters--please visit web site http://historymatters.gmu.edu resources teaching u.s. history. date: sat, 1 dec 2001 23:16:54 -0600 reply-to: american indians forum sender: american indians forum bruce mitzit subject: re: americanindiansforum digest 2 jun 2001 1 dec 2001 (#2001-31) comments: cc: automatic digest processor in-reply-to: <20011202050416.bimn1594.mailhost.ind.ameritech.net@listserv.cuny.edu> mime-version: 1.0 content-type: text/plain; charset="us-ascii" format="flowed" seventy-five dollars rent movie strikes unconscionably stiff. books price buy one, get keep shame attractive movie little-seen hardly known the---i going say 'prohibitive'---risible expectation distributor. bruce mitzit manager information systems university chicago laboratory schools office: 773-834-1866 fax: 773-702-7455 12:00 -0500 12/2/01, automatic digest processor wrote: one message totalling 57 lines issue. >topics day: 1. wmm new release: kinaalda forum sponsored history matters--please visit web >site http://historymatters.gmu.edu resources >teaching u.s. history. >date: fri, 30 nov 2001 16:51:36 -0500 educate >subject: wmm new release: kinaalda wmm new release >women make movies proud announce new release kinaalda: navajo >rite passage lena carr. kinaalda ceremony intricate four-day event performed guide >young girls' ascent womanhood. unique documentary, navajo >filmmaker lena carr journeys back childhood chronicling >13-year old niece's initiation womanhood. festival international du film d'amiens, amiens, france santa fe film festival american indian film festival museum american indian film festival multi-layered film elegantly weaves carr's complex personal >story, family community history, backdrop u.s. >government policy toward navajo... compelling work beginning >end." elaine charnov, margaret mead film

content: home many pasts evidence www.history blackboard reference talking history syllabi students teachers puzzle us search site back talking history results back archive list date: mon, 3 apr 2000 10:07:53 -0400 reply-to: forum asian american history sender: forum asian american history jack tchen subject: opening statement greetings! academic historian interested better ways teaching engaging students public historian believes collaborate various publics exploring importance historical narratives everyday life. i'm open serving something resource researching teaching materials related asians americas (yes, think important hemispheric framing) public humanities dicussions linkages form schools, universities, community-based organizations,and various publics. must also note asian american related history usually covered u.s. history survey courses hope month provide forum address ways think asian american history within context survey course. first foremost, i'd like learn who's i'd especially interested interested students writing experiences and/or oral histories, research, documentation, fieldwork. thought background asian american studies would useful field asian american studies exploding late lots new research, faculty appointments, grants, exhibitions, videos, new published studies. building field's origins community-based scholarship example, see article mr. mark lai, chronicle higher education), key moment quality quantity studies reaching critical mass. issues historical content, who's included term "asian american," pacific islanders included? (yes, think reason need understand shift atlantic world making europeanized euro-u.s. pacific world), periodization believe go back manila-acapulco trade), intergroup social relations, whiteness, etc. addressed, issues form infrastructure historical knowledge production, problems lack archives local historical societies. good portion growth field asian american history. lots graduate students emerging history departments across country fair number history hires past years. indeed, association asian american studies (aaas) formed history caucus conference last year. field also dynamic public dimension videomakers, writers, curators, artists working established arts humanities institutions community-based efforts. much interesting research continues generated public community-based sites. (i'd happy share quick list asian american museums video/film distributors, example.) another growth area among teachers high school college levels) worked students writing lives documenting aspect family and/or community stories. book susan kuklin called _speaking out!_ excellent example. many us asian american studies believe need work broader interdisciplinary area historical studies. historical studies i'm referring scholars english literature lisa lowe uc-san diego wrote _immigrant acts_) journalism/oral testimony anne fadiman, freelance journalist, wrote _spirit catches fall down_ hmong merced california conflicting cross cultural health care practices) substantially deal consider defining characteristic historical studies-that generative dialogue critical understandings present past. finally, study asian/pacific americans also study cultural formation u.s. identity. expropriation native american land enslavement african americans fundamental political economy u.s., trade "orient" fundamental. u.s. self deeply shaped otherness various forms racialized "primitivism," self formulated "orientalism." mind, inclusion asian/pacific american history pluralizes improves o

title: Talking History Listserv
content: home many pasts evidence www.history blackboard reference talking history syllabi students teachers puzzle us search site back talking history results back archive list date: tue, 2 may 2000 07:15:16 -0400 reply-to: forum asian american history sender: forum asian american history john kuo wei tchen subject: loni ding's "ancestors america" video series mime-version: 1.0 content-type: text/plain; charset="us-ascii" veteran video maker loni ding process editing third three part history series called "ancestors americas. say upfront helped work believe useful, serious effort reframe history asians u.s. within much broader look formation americas 16th century onwards. included part one "coolies, sailors, settlers" manila acapulco galleon trade brief history indentured servitude south asians chinese "coolies" caribbean, south, central america. i've worked number filmmakers never worked one thorough thoughtful research. single handedly dug photograph mountain peruvian (chincha islands) guano (sea bird droppings) mined chinese shipped u.s. ships fertilize u.s. southern tobacco plantations. history know i'd curious used worked. way new cut edited responding much feedback gotten.) part two extremely well crafted focuses "chinese american west." includes visits sacred, largely forgotten, historical sites tangibly illustrate continued ways chinese american labor lasting impact landscape--far standard railroads gold mine scenes. you'll see plantings, terraced mountainsides, remote shrines, etc. part three subsequent migrations 20th century. pbs picked national telecast log website check videos teacher's guide. professor john kuo wei tchen a/p/a studies program institute new york university 269 mercer street #609 new york, new york 10003 phone: 212/998-3695 fax: 212/995-4705 www.apa.nyu.edu please note email address change: jack.tchen@nyu.edu date: tue, 2 may 2000 09:38:18 -0400 reply-to: forum asian american history sender: forum asian american history randy kaplan subject: re: bloodhound gang mime-version: 1.0 content-type: text/plain; charset="us-ascii" dear colleagues, done internet surfing come bloodhound gang recorded "yellow fever" cd entitled "one fierce beer coaster" released december 1996 uni/geffen. write geffen records 9130 sunset blvd los angeles, ca 90069-6197. group also scheduled appear utah state university may 17. george h. emmert president college; phone number 435-797-1157. rose marie ernstrom assistant pres., phone number. president mote's e-mail univ. maryland dmote@deans.umd.edu also list jessie davies charge special events, e-mail jdavies1@accmail.umd.edu hope helps folks stop hatred. randy kaplan school performing arts suny geneseo date: tue, 2 may 2000 15:41:31 -0400 reply-to: forum asian american history sender: forum asian american history randy kaplan subject: information re. bloodhound gang comments: an1@geneseo.edu, qk1@geneseo.edu, mwh3@geneseo.edu, jyl2@geneseo.edu, mly99@geneseo.edu, jn3@geneseo.edu, les1@geneseo.edu, crm2@geneseo.edu, dern08@aol.com, hnt1@geneseo.edu, lw1@geneseo.edu, bws1@geneseo.edu, scc2@geneseo.edu, atwell@hws.edu, barlow@u.washington.edu, wrbauer7@aol.com, bsu@geneseo.edu, burkman@acsu.buffalo.edu, lmburns@english.umass.edu, owner-aaascommunity@lists.uoregon.edu, panasian@aol.com, pcc1@geneseo.edu, ac5@geneseo.edu, michaelc@usc.edu, mcole@oswego.edu, cac11@cornell.edu, ruth.mannes@stmartins.com, tdang@eastwestplayers.com, davisr@usfca.edu, kdrury@hearst.com, sedlin@kpbx.org, jle3@geneseo.edu, ferrellc@earthlink.net, rlforman1@aol.com, mjf@cdog.com, kag5@geneseo.edu, slgdesig@swbell.net, joeozu@aol.com, jgraves@gevatheatre.org, agoeldner@gevatheatre.org, wolfgriv@aol.com, gross@hws.edu, guerena@library.ucsb.edu, shee@eastwestplayers.com, stefanie.henkin@mtv.com, hug@geneseo.edu, jacksopk@stream.mcs.muohio.edu, spjain@earthlink.net, johnsonm@augsburg.edu, rojo30@juno.com, kaplan@cs.clemson.edu, karvetski@mindspring.com, kasa@geneseo.edu, gccc@geneseo.edu, sava22@hotmail.com, fightcrimeny

boundary="----=_nextpart_000_00d5_01bfabf4.ea6ac920" multi-part message mime format. ------=_nextpart_000_00d5_01bfabf4.ea6ac920 content-type: text/plain; charset="iso-8859-1" content-transfer-encoding: quoted-printable congress classroom=ae congress classroom=ae national, award-winning education program ninth year. sponsored dirksen congressional center,pekin, il, cooperation bradley university, peoria, il, dedicated exchange ideas information teaching congress. =20 congress classroom=ae designed secondary school teachers junior community college faculty teach u.s. history, government civics, political science, social studies. 30 35 teachers selected year take part program. =20 2000 program emphasize upcoming presidential congressional elections, along general overview congress, blending historical contemporary sessions. participants also gain experience center's educational web site, congresslink (http://www.congresslink.org/), features online access lesson plans, student activities, historical materials, related web sites, subject matter experts. throughout program, participants work national experts well colleagues across midwest. combination first-hand knowledge peer-to-peer interaction give participants new ideas, materials, professionally enriching experience. =20 workshop held july 30 august 2, 2000, campus bradley university peoria, illinois. session presenters topics include: jeffrey bernstein, assistant professor political science, eastern michigan university.=20 session: "testing classroom campaign simulation"=20 mary alice erickson, former staffer campaign manager robert h. michel. =20 session: "organizing campaign house seat"=20 stephen frantzich, professor chair, department political science, u.s. naval academy. sessions: "citizen courage cynical age" watch campaign commercials campaign events"=20 =20 roderick p. hart, shivers chair communication government university texas, austin, director t

participation congress classroomâ® 2001 congress classroomâ® national, award-winning education program tenth year. sponsored dirksen congressional center located pekin, illinois cooperation bradley university, peoria, illinois, dedicated exchange ideas information teaching congress. congress classroomâ® designed secondary school teachers junior community college faculty teach u.s. history, government civics, political science, social studies. 30 35 teachers selected year take part program. 2001 program theme legislative process." individual sessions offered topics, among others: lobbying, committees, demographics membership house senate, policy agenda new congress, relationship president congress, exercise leadership congress. also gain experience center's educational web site, "congresslink" (www.congresslink.org), features online access lesson plans, student activities, historical materials, related web sites, subject matter experts. throughout program work national experts well colleagues across nation. combination first-hand knowledge peer-to-peer interaction give new ideas, materials, professionally enriching experience. workshop held august 5-8, 2001, campus title: Talking History ListservRe: Did Hoover create the New Deal?PWANew Deal vs. "common sense"
content: home many pasts evidence www.history blackboard reference talking history syllabi students teachers puzzle us search site back talking history results back archive list date: mon, 2 apr 2001 16:25:00 -0400 reply-to: great depression new deal history forum sender: great depression new deal history forum ellen noonan subject: opening statement alan brinkley mime-version: 1.0 content-transfer-encoding: quoted-printable content-type: text/plain; charset=iso-8859-1 dear forum subscribers, grateful opportunity participate forum teaching great depression new deal. teaching writing field many years, i=92m sure least participants forum well. hope conversations emerge month also help scholars teachers new period american history. purpose forum, see help us find new ways thinking period remained largely contained within single, powerful paradigm generation. one might trace origins paradigm arthur schlesinger=92s three volumes age roosevelt (1958-1961) william leuchtenburg=92s franklin d. roosevelt new deal (1963), still widely used teach 1930s. offers picture nation encountering unparalleled social economic crisis; ineffectual government response crisis hoover administration; growing radicalism dissidence instability; arrival triumphant new deal restored confidence, stopped downward spiral, created important new national programs institutions, reshaped american politics make democrats dominant party liberalism shaped new deal dominant creed three decades. idea "new deal order" still frames way think past present politics. need repudiate paradigm, helps explain many things 1930s, argue great deal 1930s first, new deal =96 many lives contained many ideals, whose historical image often flattened impulses prevailed survived remain visible. lost impulses new deal; once-live options ultimately abandoned; vivid imaginings bold experiments ultimately came naught; process things competed became enduring legacies roosevelt years: important still incomplete part history 1930s deserves attention. even important however, many aspects social, cultural, intellectual, even political life america 1930s effectively represented new deal-centered scholarship dominated field. significant new scholarly work recent years impact great depression many previously unexplored areas. scholars examined radicals, conservatives, dissidents opposed =96 offered political alternatives =96 new deal. significant new histories african americans, native americans, latinos, minorities simultaneously beneficiaries victims policies roosevelt administration. rise new forms cultural history brought attention patterns popular culture survival consumerist values midst great economic dislocation. labor historians chronicled emergence modern labor movement response depression new deal labor laws, also transformation working-class culture. demographic environmental historians studied vast migrations, depleted regions (notably dust bowl agrarian south) helped launch others new demographic economic era (california, particular). impact depression communities, lives families, role women, children also subject interesting literature. artistic literary life 1930s spawned significant scholarly attention. american communist party popular front emerged major factors reinterpretation era. studying era, opposed examining theme field =96 teaching 1930s opposed teaching labor history history race ethnicity history labor =96 presents special challenges special opportunities. one hand, literally nothing happened 1930s potentially relevant

title:
Talking History ListservOpening Statement from Estelle
FreedmanRe: Opening Statement from Estelle FreedmanRe: feminism and women's
historyRe: groundbreaking feminist?Re: groundbreaking feminist?
content: home many pasts evidence www.history blackboard reference talking history syllabi students teachers puzzle us search site back talking history results back archive list date: sun, 1 sep 2002 19:04:47 -0400 reply-to: "history feminist movements u.s." sender: "history feminist movements u.s." kelly schrum subject: opening statement estelle freedman mime-version: 1.0 content-type: multipart/alternative; boundary="boundary_(id_im4ykbqf+3z1wfsbvir0aa)" --boundary_(id_im4ykbqf+3z1wfsbvir0aa) content-type: text/plain; charset=us-ascii; format=flowed content-transfer-encoding: 7bit dear colleagues, welcome forum "feminist movements u.s. history." look forward sharing teaching strategies subject. introduction identify three challenges welcome others well. first, define feminism narrowly, include prime, public moments seneca falls, suffrage, women's liberation, broadly, include women's reform movements ranging social purity pacifism, birth control arts? second, integrate feminism mainstream u.s. history teaching? third, past forums (e.g. religious history), students bring classroom diverse personal political perspectives. navigate among use history sharpen analytic skills, convey knowledge, encourage open-minded discussion differences? 1. teaching, first acknowledge historically specific origins feminism. term originated late nineteenth century europe came use u.s. early twentieth century younger, radical wing suffrage movement; given association national woman's party's single-minded focus equal rights amendment, exclusion broader social justice agenda, activists claimed label 1960s.) warn students use feminism umbrella term cover movements activists may called feminists time. also present working definition allows approach broader historical terrain: feminism belief women men inherently equal worth. societies privilege men group, social movements necessary achieve equality women men, understanding gender always intersects social hierarchies. turning back, p. 7) another way phrase definition individual group effort empower women whether personally, economically, politically contribute feminist history; bottom line belief equal worth rejection hierarchy justifies male authority women. 2. one major emphasis teaching way political economic history u.s. produced feminist responses; means history feminism integral larger story american life. one, feminism rests upon democratic principles self-determination gradually extended beyond initial white, male reference point include rights african americans women. equally important, shift family economies wage labor gradually drew women work force, encountered (along minorities) second class status, well double duty household labor. response gendered limits democratic politics economic opportunity, feminism articulated goal full political economic citizenship women. historical questions raise developments include: social movements women's equality arise theories strategies feminists develop? women address, women, men, attract? opposes race gender politics influenced cooperation conflict? feminist politics changed time example, arguments based biological difference maternal authority predominate, arguments based political ideals equal rights predominate? intersect?) broad overview, talk locate feminist history general courses. example: impact american revolution women's consciousness rights? role literacy educational institutions play creating feminist leaders movements? certainly lack opportunity. benevolence involved? racism? resistance? role economic interest? united states change form substance imperial dominance moved nineteenth century twentieth?= 1900, united states seek forms influence =96 protectorates cuba panama; financial receiverships dominican republic, nicaragua, haiti, liberia; kinds informal economic, cultural, military connections =96 considered examples "imperialism"? imperial policies interact domestic political, economic, cultural history? arguments techniques mobilized favor empire mobilized dynamics impact political cultural contests =96- united states= territories influenced united states=92s role t= world? consider questions, becomes illuminating think word "imperialism" =96 =96 used different people. migh= t various meanings discursive contexts shape different answers questions frame different narratives "american imperialism"? goal forum settle answers questions discuss teachers might spark enlightened classroom conversations around questions. assume wish get students involved diverse readings period well promote critical engagement influential historical interpretations. let=92s share works. teaching materials proved especially useful? (www.mtholyoke.edu/acad/intrel/to1914.htm); "internet modern history sourcebook" (www.fordham.edu/halsall/mod/modsbook34.htm); "instances use united states forces abroad, 1798-1993." (www.history.navy.mil/wars/foabroad.htm); "wars conflicts united states navy" (www.history.navy.mil/wars/index.html) james jandrowitz wrote: william blum two recent useful books: killing hope: u.s.militar cia interventions since world war ii; recent book: rogue state: guide world's superpower. happy reading, certainly get students' attention. original message nancy nevins date: tuesday, march 20, 2001 1:42 pm subject: re: book imperialism take look scott cook "colonial encounters age high imperialism" 1996. longman world history series. excellent job looking zone interaction, well topics colonizing technologies women colonizers colonized. nancy nevins james jandrowitz wrote: go webpage information books speeches. grover furr teaches montclair state university also good webpage many related links sources. cbgord@aol.com wrote: michael parenti's empire, published 1995, excellent. don't publisher's name handy. write need craig gordon message dated 3/9/01 12:56:10 pm pacific standard time, mickydiez@yahoo.com writes: anybody inform names current books written imperialism past 2 5 years? books important articles imperialism globalization. currently research document university graduate program interested finding bibliography available either italian, english spanish.
